threats.title: AgentDAO Vacuous Proposal Success When Token Total Supply Is Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentDAO.state() returns ProposalState.Succeeded when forVotes == getPastTotalSupply(snapshot). When totalSupply at snapshot block is 0 (new agent, no delegations), the condition 0 == 0 is trivially true, allowing any Active proposal to be considered Succeeded and immediately executed. The ContributionNft admin bypasses the proposal threshold check, so they can create and execute arbitrary proposals against any agent DAO with 0 delegated supply.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct logic flaw in the state override that creates a vacuous truth condition. Any newly created agent DAO is vulnerable until tokens are delegated. Attacker can drain the agent's TBA or modify governance settings.

proof_of_signal.pattern: forVotes == token().getPastTotalSupply(proposalSnapshot(proposalId)) returns true when both are 0

preconditions: Agent token exists with 0 delegated supply at the proposal snapshot block
ContributionNft admin address is known (or attacker meets proposal threshold)
Agent DAO holds assets or controls valuable state

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: state() override does not guard against 0 == 0 vacuous truth
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: ContributionNft admin bypasses threshold, enabling proposal creation even with 0 votes
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Window exists from agent creation until first token delegation

operational_questions: How long after agent creation are tokens typically delegated?
What assets are held in new agent TBAs at creation time?
Can execute() be called directly when state() returns Succeeded?
validation_requirements: Confirm OZ Governor.execute checks state == Succeeded
Verify getPastTotalSupply returns 0 for new agents
Test proposal creation and execution with 0 supply

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for new agent creation events from AgentFactory
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Create malicious proposal targeting the agent DAO before tokens are delegated
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call execute() immediately since state() returns Succeeded (0 == 0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Drain TBA assets or modify governance parameters via executed proposal

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Agent TBA drained, governance parameters maliciously modified
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Irreversible if TBA funds are stolen
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All new agent DAOs until tokens are delegated

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: 0 (ContributionNft admin bypass or threshold met trivially)
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Immediately after agent creation, before delegation

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: New agent creation without immediate delegation
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Proposal execution on agent DAOs with 0 total supply

threats.title: FERC20 burnFrom Does Not Decrement totalSupply Breaking Supply Conservation
threats.hypothesis_description: FERC20.burnFrom and _burn only decrement _balances[user] without decrementing _totalSupply. During graduation in _openTradingOnUniswap, burnFrom is called on pair holdings, but totalSupply remains inflated. The stored _token.data.supply (original totalSupply) is passed to executeBondingCurveApplication to determine agent token supply parameters. This creates a mismatch: agent tokens are minted based on inflated FERC20 supply, but actual FERC20 circulation is lower. The 1:1 unwrapToken exchange rate becomes incorrect.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct code defect in FERC20's burn implementation that propagates through the graduation flow, affecting agent token supply calculations and unwrap economics.

proof_of_signal.pattern: burnFrom reduces _balances[user] but never decrements _totalSupply; _burn at lines 98-100 same issue

preconditions: Any FERC20 token launched via Bonding reaches graduation threshold
burnFrom is called during graduation (always happens in _openTradingOnUniswap)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _totalSupply never decremented in burn functions; executeBondingCurveApplication receives inflated supply

operational_questions: What ratio does executeBondingCurveApplication use between totalSupply and lpSupply?
How much excess agent token supply is created vs actual FERC20 in circulation?
Can the supply mismatch be exploited to extract more agent tokens than entitled?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact supply discrepancy for a graduation scenario
Verify unwrapToken 1:1 mapping with inflated supply
Test agent token creation with correct vs inflated parameters

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Buy tokens to trigger graduation threshold
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Graduation creates agent tokens with inflated supply parameters
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit supply mismatch in unwrapToken or agent token distribution

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All graduated tokens have incorrect supply accounting
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot fix without contract upgrade; all historical graduations affected
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Token graduation, unwrap, agent token economics

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: FERC20 totalSupply vs sum of balances divergence post-burn
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Agent token supply exceeding actual FERC20 circulation

threats.title: Bonding.launch() Permanently Broken Due to Double NonReentrant Guard
threats.hypothesis_description: Bonding.launch() has the nonReentrant modifier and internally calls launchFor() which also has nonReentrant. Since both share the same ReentrancyGuardUpgradeable storage, calling launch() always reverts because the guard is already set when launchFor() is entered. This forces all token launches to go through launchFor() directly, which accepts an arbitrary 'creator' parameter that anyone can set to any address, enabling creator attribution manipulation.
threats.investigation_rationale: launch() is dead code. launchFor() being the only working path means msg.sender != creator is possible, allowing attackers to attribute token creation to arbitrary addresses, affecting downstream creator fee distribution in AgentTax.

proof_of_signal.pattern: launch() has nonReentrant and calls launchFor() which also has nonReentrant; nested guard triggers revert

preconditions: Any user attempts to call launch() - always reverts
For creator manipulation: attacker calls launchFor with arbitrary creator address

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: launch() wraps launchFor() but both carry nonReentrant modifier

operational_questions: Is launch() used by any frontend or integration?
What downstream impact does false creator attribution have on AgentTax fees?
Can attackers steal creator tax revenue by attributing popular tokens to themselves?
validation_requirements: Confirm launch() reverts in all cases
Verify launchFor() creator parameter propagates to graduation and tax
Test creator fee redirection via false attribution

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Call launchFor() with attacker-controlled address as 'creator'
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Token gains traction and reaches graduation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: AgentTax creator fees route to attacker instead of real creator
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Capital for launch fee and initial purchase

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: launch() is permanently broken; creator attribution is unreliable
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix both issues
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Token launch, creator identification, tax distribution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: All launches through launchFor(), none through launch()
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Creator addresses that don't match actual launchers
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Multiple tokens attributed to same popular creator address

threats.title: Deliberate Vote Type Enables Strategic Double-Voting in Governance
threats.hypothesis_description: In GovernorCountingSimpleUpgradeable._countVote, voting with support=3 (Deliberate) returns early WITHOUT setting hasVoted[account]=true. A voter can first cast a Deliberate vote (which emits VoteCast event revealing their voting power), then cast a real vote (For/Against/Abstain) since hasVoted is still false. This enables strategic voting: observe others' votes before committing, while appearing to have participated.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Deliberate vote type creates an asymmetric information advantage. In contentious proposals, large holders can signal participation without commitment, then vote strategically after seeing the vote distribution.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (support == uint8(VoteType.Deliberate)) { return; } — exits before setting hasVoted[account] = true

preconditions: Voter holds governance tokens with delegated voting power
Active proposal exists in any DAO using these counting modules

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Deliberate vote returns before marking hasVoted, allowing second vote
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any active proposal voting period

operational_questions: Does the VoteCast event for Deliberate expose voting weight?
How do off-chain governance UIs handle duplicate vote events?
In AgentDAO, does the score tracking handle this correctly?
validation_requirements: Cast Deliberate vote, verify hasVoted is false, cast real vote
Verify both VoteCast events are emitted
Check if AgentDAO score is double-incremented

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Cast Deliberate vote on contentious proposal to reveal participation without commitment
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Observe other voters' positions from on-chain events
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Cast real vote (For/Against/Abstain) strategically based on observed distribution

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Undermines governance integrity across all DAOs
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix _countVote logic

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Any amount of delegated voting power

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Same address casting VoteCast twice on same proposal
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Deliberate votes followed by real votes from same address
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large holders consistently voting Deliberate first

threats.title: AgentTax dcaSell Uses Incorrect Slippage Calculation Based on Input Amount
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentTax.dcaSell calculates minOutput as (amountToSwap * (DENOM - slippage)) / DENOM, which applies slippage percentage to the INPUT token amount rather than the EXPECTED OUTPUT amount. Since taxToken and assetToken likely have very different market values, this calculation is fundamentally wrong. If taxToken is worth much less than assetToken, the minOutput will be impossibly high (DoS). If taxToken is worth much more, minOutput provides no meaningful slippage protection.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a mathematical error in the slippage formula that either causes permanent DoS on tax collection or removes all slippage protection, enabling value extraction through market manipulation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: minOutput = ((amountToSwap * (DENOM - slippage)) / DENOM) uses input amount as basis instead of expected output from router.getAmountsOut

preconditions: EXECUTOR_ROLE calls dcaSell (normal operation)
taxToken and assetToken have different market prices (highly likely)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Slippage applied to wrong denominator - input amount vs expected output
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: EXECUTOR_ROLE triggers but the code bug is inherent

operational_questions: What is the actual price ratio between taxToken and assetToken?
Does dcaSell currently succeed or always revert?
How much value is at risk in accumulated tax swaps?
validation_requirements: Calculate actual minOutput vs correct minOutput for real token prices
Test dcaSell with actual token pair to determine if DoS or no-protection mode
Compare with handleAgentTaxes which uses router.getAmountsOut correctly

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: If minOutput is too low (no protection): manipulate pool price before dcaSell
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Executor calls dcaSell with seemingly safe slippage parameter
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Swap executes at manipulated price, extracting value from tax funds
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Restore pool price and profit from the difference
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Capital for pool manipulation; must be in same block or adjacent blocks

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Tax collection permanently broken (DoS) or tax funds drained via manipulation
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade; accumulated tax funds at risk
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All agent tax swaps via dcaSell path

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium - requires understanding of AMM manipulation
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When dcaSell is called by executor

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: dcaSell transactions consistently reverting or executing with bad rates
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Tax swap amounts significantly below market rate
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large pool liquidity changes around dcaSell transactions

threats.title: FGenesis.createGenesis Permissionless Grants BONDING_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3
threats.hypothesis_description: FGenesis.createGenesis has no access control - any address that pays the fee can call it. Each call deploys a new Genesis contract and grants it BONDING_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3 via grantRole. FGenesis has DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3 (confirmed by deployment script). This means any fee-paying user can spawn unlimited Genesis contracts each holding BONDING_ROLE, expanding the attack surface on AgentFactoryV3 and potentially exhausting role management.
threats.investigation_rationale: While Genesis contracts are constrained by FACTORY_ROLE for their critical functions, the unlimited granting of BONDING_ROLE to new contracts without access control violates least-privilege. If AgentFactoryV3 has additional BONDING_ROLE-gated functions, each Genesis contract could potentially abuse them.
threats.config_evidence: genesis/deployGenesis.ts grants DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on AgentFactory to FGenesis

proof_of_signal.pattern: No access control modifier on createGenesis; grants BONDING_ROLE to new contract via AgentFactoryV3.grantRole

preconditions: Attacker has enough VIRTUAL tokens to pay creation fee
FGenesis has DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No role check on createGenesis; unlimited BONDING_ROLE grants

operational_questions: What functions are gated by BONDING_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3?
Can Genesis contracts directly call AgentFactoryV3 BONDING_ROLE functions outside of onGenesisSuccess?
What is the creation fee amount vs potential exploit value?
validation_requirements: Review full AgentFactoryV3 for all BONDING_ROLE-gated functions
Verify Genesis contracts cannot directly call agentFactory beyond onGenesisSuccess
Test mass creation of Genesis contracts and role enumeration

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Pay creation fee to spawn Genesis contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Genesis contract receives BONDING_ROLE on AgentFactoryV3
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If AgentFactoryV3 has exploitable BONDING_ROLE functions, call them from Genesis
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat to create multiple attack vectors

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Potential access to AgentFactoryV3 BONDING_ROLE functions
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unlimited role grants expanding attack surface
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot easily revoke BONDING_ROLE from all created contracts

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: High volume of createGenesis calls from single address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpectedly many BONDING_ROLE holders on AgentFactory

threats.title: Genesis Per-Transaction Cap Bypass via Multiple Participate Calls
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis.participate checks virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount per transaction but does not enforce a cumulative per-user cap. mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] += virtualsAmt accumulates without bound. A whale can call participate() repeatedly, each time contributing up to maxContributionVirtualAmount, dominating the genesis event and receiving a disproportionate share of agent tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The maxContributionVirtualAmount check is per-call, defeating its purpose of fair distribution. This is a logic error in the cap enforcement.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount) checks per-tx, but mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] += virtualsAmt has no cumulative cap

preconditions: Genesis is in Active state
Attacker has sufficient VIRTUAL tokens
maxContributionVirtualAmount is set (intended as meaningful cap)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Per-tx check instead of cumulative per-user check

operational_questions: What is the intended cap behavior - per-tx or per-user?
How does the off-chain allocation algorithm handle dominant contributors?
Can the cap be enforced by checking mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] + virtualsAmt?
validation_requirements: Call participate twice to exceed maxContributionVirtualAmount cumulatively
Verify no cumulative check exists
Assess impact on token distribution fairness

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Dominate genesis allocation based on contribution proportion

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot undo contributions; off-chain allocation may mitigate

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Single address with many participate transactions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated small contributions from same address

threats.title: ContributionNft Mintable for Unaccepted or Rejected Proposals
threats.hypothesis_description: ContributionNft.mint only verifies msg.sender == personaDAO.proposalProposer(proposalId) but does NOT check if the proposal has succeeded. The isAccepted() function exists but is not enforced in mint(). A proposer can mint ContributionNfts for active, defeated, or cancelled proposals, polluting the contribution tree and parent-child relationships used by ServiceNft.
threats.investigation_rationale: The missing state check allows rejected contributions to be permanently recorded on-chain, potentially confusing downstream systems that rely on ContributionNft data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only checks msg.sender == proposalProposer; no call to isAccepted() or state check

preconditions: Proposer has created a proposal in an AgentDAO
Proposal exists in any state (active, defeated, cancelled)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: isAccepted exists as view function but not enforced in mint
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any proposer can exploit their own proposals

operational_questions: Do downstream systems (ServiceNft, rewards) check isAccepted before processing?
Can spurious ContributionNfts affect maturity/impact calculations?
Does the parent-child tree validation depend on acceptance status?
validation_requirements: Create proposal, vote it down, then mint ContributionNft
Check if ServiceNft.mint can be called for rejected contributions
Verify impact on contribution tree integrity

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Proposal gets rejected but ContributionNft persists
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Polluted contribution tree affects downstream operations

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Contribution tree pollution, potential downstream impacts
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: NFTs are permanent; cannot be burned without owner consent

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ContributionNfts for proposals that never succeeded
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Mismatched proposal states vs minted contributions

threats.title: Bonding.buy() and sell() Lack Reentrancy Protection at Bonding Level
threats.hypothesis_description: Bonding.buy() and sell() do not have nonReentrant modifiers. While router functions they call have nonReentrant, the Bonding-level state updates (tokenInfo data: price, marketCap, liquidity, volume) occur AFTER external calls to router.buy/sell. If the asset token or traded token has transfer hooks (ERC777, hooks-enabled ERC20), a callback could re-enter Bonding functions reading stale tokenInfo state.
threats.investigation_rationale: The checks-effects-interactions pattern is violated. State updates happen after external calls, creating a window for reentrancy if tokens have callbacks.
deployment_assumptions: Asset token or traded tokens must have transfer hooks/callbacks (ERC777-like)
threats.config_evidence: Depends on which token is configured as assetToken in FRouter

proof_of_signal.pattern: router.buy() called at line ~185, tokenInfo updates at lines 187-200 occur after external call

preconditions: Asset token or FERC20 token has transfer callbacks (ERC777-like)
Callback allows re-entry into Bonding.buy, sell, or unwrapToken

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State updates after external calls without reentrancy guard
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every buy/sell transaction with callback-enabled tokens

operational_questions: Does the configured asset token support transfer hooks?
Can unwrapToken be called during a buy callback before state update?
What stale data could be read during reentrancy?
validation_requirements: Check asset token for ERC777/callback support
Attempt reentrancy via token transfer callback
Assess state inconsistency from stale reads

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify if asset token has transfer hooks
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft token with transfer callback that re-enters Bonding
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: During buy/sell, callback reads stale tokenInfo before update
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Exploit stale state for price manipulation or incorrect graduation trigger
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Must deploy or use callback-enabled token

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Price manipulation, incorrect graduation, state corruption

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Complex transactions with internal calls to Bonding

threats.title: AgentDAO.getMaturity Division by Zero Blocks ServiceNft Minting
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentDAO.getMaturity divides _proposalMaturities[proposalId] by forVotes without a zero check. If a proposal reaches execution state with 0 forVotes (possible via the vacuous 0==0 success path or edge cases), calling getMaturity reverts. ServiceNft.mint calls getMaturity, so the service NFT cannot be minted, permanently blocking the contribution from being registered.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a cascading failure: the vacuous success bug (OPS-H001) combined with the division by zero creates a permanent DoS on ServiceNft minting for affected proposals.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _proposalMaturities[proposalId] / forVotes with no zero check on forVotes

preconditions: Proposal reaches Succeeded state with 0 forVotes
ServiceNft.mint is called for this proposal (via DAO execution)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unchecked division by forVotes which can be 0

operational_questions: Under what conditions can forVotes be 0 when proposal is Succeeded?
Does this permanently block the contribution registration?
Are there other callers of getMaturity?
validation_requirements: Create proposal, achieve Succeeded state with 0 forVotes
Call getMaturity and verify revert
Attempt ServiceNft.mint and verify cascade failure

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create proposal on agent DAO with 0 total supply (see OPS-H001)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Execute proposal (state shows Succeeded due to 0==0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attempt ServiceNft.mint — reverts due to division by zero in getMaturity

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent blocking of contribution registration for affected proposals
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to add zero check

threats.title: Genesis onGenesisSuccess Repeatable Call Overwrites User Claimable Agent Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis.onGenesisSuccess can be called multiple times after the first launch because there is no 'isSucceeded' terminal flag. The only guard (refundUserCountForFailed == 0) remains true after success. claimableAgentTokens uses assignment (=) not addition (+=), so a second call overwrites unclaimed allocations. The OPERATION_ROLE operator could redirect all unclaimed tokens by calling with different distribution arrays.
threats.investigation_rationale: The lack of terminal success state combined with overwrite semantics creates a critical vulnerability exploitable by the OPERATION_ROLE holder (backend ops wallet).

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimableAgentTokens[distributeAgentTokenUserAddresses[i]] = distributeAgentTokenUserAmounts[i] — assignment overwrites, no terminal flag prevents re-call

preconditions: Genesis has completed first successful onGenesisSuccess call
Users have not yet claimed all agent tokens
OPERATION_ROLE holder calls onGenesisSuccess again with different distribution

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No isSucceeded flag; no guard against re-entry post-success; = vs += on claimable
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After first success until all users claim

operational_questions: Is the OPERATION_ROLE wallet a multisig?
What is the typical delay between distribution and user claims?
Can a isSucceeded flag be added retroactively?
validation_requirements: Call onGenesisSuccess twice with different distributions
Verify second call overwrites first call's claimable amounts
Confirm users who hadn't claimed lose their allocation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: OPERATION_ROLE calls onGenesisSuccess with legitimate distribution
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for some users to not claim
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: OPERATION_ROLE calls onGenesisSuccess again with attacker-controlled addresses in distribution
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker claims redirected tokens; original users' claims zeroed or reduced

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete control over genesis token distribution
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: User funds stolen; trust in genesis mechanism destroyed

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After first onGenesisSuccess, before user claims

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple onGenesisSuccess calls for same genesis ID

threats.title: Genesis withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized Drains Unclaimed Agent Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis.withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized allows DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE (FGenesis factory) to withdraw ANY ERC20 token after finalization, including the agent token that users haven't claimed yet. There is no accounting check to protect unclaimed claimableAgentTokens balances. The FGenesis ADMIN_ROLE can call this via FGenesis.withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized to drain agent tokens before users claim them.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function lacks protection for reserved user claims. While intended for leftover dust, it can extract the entire agent token balance including amounts allocated to users.
threats.config_evidence: genesis/deployGenesis.ts grants ADMIN_ROLE to process.env.ADMIN for FGenesis

proof_of_signal.pattern: No check that withdrawn amount preserves sum(claimableAgentTokens); can withdraw agentTokenAddress entirely

preconditions: Genesis has been finalized (success/fail/cancel)
Users have unclaimed agent tokens
ADMIN_ROLE holder calls withdraw with token=agentTokenAddress

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No reserved balance check against sum of claimableAgentTokens
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After finalization until all users claim

operational_questions: Is ADMIN_ROLE a multisig with proper governance?
How long do users typically take to claim?
Can a reserved balance check be added?
validation_requirements: Set up claimable tokens, then withdraw via admin
Verify claimAgentToken reverts after withdrawal
Calculate maximum extractable amount

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for successful genesis with agent token distribution
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All unclaimed agent tokens transferred to admin-specified address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users who call claimAgentToken get revert due to insufficient balance

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Stolen tokens cannot be recovered without admin cooperation
